old_claim.note: Human Anatomy & Physiology provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim_absent: No direct dependency remains because Leonardo's late-15th-century sfumato and chiaroscuro practice predates the 1543 Vesalian anatomy node and is sourced as a painting/light-shadow technique, not as an anatomy technology.

source_shape.source_locator: Britannica defines chiaroscuro as the representation of light and shadow to define three-dimensional objects and says Leonardo brought it to full potential in European painting in the late 15th century.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source dates the corrected technique to Leonardo's late-15th-century painting practice, before the graph's 1543 anatomy node.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: The chronology and source scope make human_anatomy_physiology a later scientific context rather than a direct prerequisite for the painting technique.

ontology_before: Sfumato & Chiaroscuro depended on Renaissance human anatomy, forcing a 1543 date and laundering anatomical science into a painting-technique node.
ontology_after: Leonardo Sfumato & Chiaroscuro is anchored to late-15th-century painting sources and no longer depends on the later Vesalian anatomy node.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: sfumato_chiaroscuro_techniques no longer directly depends on human_anatomy_physiology.
would_reject_if: The node is later rescoped to anatomical illustration or scientifically anatomical figure modelling.
A stronger source shows Vesalian-style anatomy was a direct prerequisite for Leonardo's sfumato or chiaroscuro technique.
The graph splits a separate anatomy-informed-figure-modelling node that should carry the anatomy dependency.
short_reason: The technique predates and does not directly require the 1543 anatomy node.
